name: Pecan Butter Crunch - (Heath Bar)
recipeIngredient: Bakers German's Dark Sweet Chocolate (8 oz)
recipeInstructions:
Preheat oven to 300 degrees F.
N.B. This recipe requires a candy thermometer. Although somewhat expensive to make, butter crunch goes far and can be used for a large number of gifts. It keeps and travels well and can be made up to two months in advance.
1. Line a large pan (such as a 15 x 10 x 1 1/2-inch jelly roll pan) with aluminum foil.
2. Divide pecans between two small baking pans (such as 9-inch cake pans), evenly spreading 1 1/2 cups in each.
3. Bake until golden brown, about 15 minutes. Turn off the oven but leave the pans inside to keep nuts warm.
4. Put butter in a heavy 2-quart saucepan and cook over low heat until melted. Stir in sugar, water, and corn syrup. Increase flame to medium-high and cook, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon, until sugar has dissolved and mixture comes to a boil (4-5 minutes).
5. Insert candy thermometer. Continue boiling at a moderate, steady rate, stirring almost constantly, until the thermometer reaches 300 degrees F (hard crack stage). Total cooking time will be approximately 20 minutes.
6. Turn off the flame under the pot and quickly stir in salt and 1 1/2 cups of pecans from one pan. Pour into the foil-lined pan using a wooden spoon to spread the mixture to the edges.
7. In the top of a double boiler (over simmering water), melt chocolate.
8. Turn off the flame and quickly spread half of the melted chocolate over the cooled toffee. Evenly sprinkle with half of the remaining pecans and pat down with a spatula.
9. Cover the pan with waxed paper, invert onto an inverted cookie sheet, and carefully invert. Peel off the foil. Spread the toffee with the remaining melted chocolate and sprinkle with the remaining nuts. Pat down with a spatula.
10. Refrigerate until firm. Break into bite-sized pieces.
11. Store in an airtight container. It will remain fresh for at least two months.
Note: Everyone I know is crazy about this candy, so I hope that you will enjoy it. It has evolved over the years, long before this type of candy was common. The original directions said only "cook until smoking". As uncertain of my ability to distinguish between smoking and burning, I worked out degrees on a candy thermometer.
